critic_raw:
CRITIQUE (Round 1):

1. [ISSUE-A] TIME_HORIZON_MISMATCH: ML model evaluated at its peak performance point in a 6-hour window; qSOFA is evaluated once at a fixed early time. Asymmetric evaluation windows inflate apparent AUROC advantage.
2. [ISSUE-B] FEATURE_AVAILABILITY_MISMATCH: Lactate/WBC features available mid-window but not at triage. The best-window evaluation includes these features; qSOFA at triage does not.
3. [ISSUE-C] TRIAGE_VS_SURVEILLANCE_BASELINE_MISALIGNMENT: The paper proposes continuous surveillance deployment but compares against a triage tool evaluated at triage time.
defender_raw:
DEFENSE (Round 1):
Clinical workflow supports these design choices. qSOFA is used at triage. Lab data arrives naturally in routine care. The model demonstrates meaningful early warning value regardless of comparison methodology.
adjudication_raw:
Round 1: All issues open. Defender appeals to workflow but does not address the methodological asymmetry.

Round 2 on all issues jointly:
Critic: Each issue creates a systematic advantage for the ML model in ways that inflate the AUROC gap: time window selection advantage, information availability advantage, and purpose misalignment. The cumulative effect on the reported 0.15 AUROC gap is substantial.
Defender: I cannot rebut the time window selection issue — it is a methodological choice that favors the ML model. The lab availability issue is real. The purpose mismatch is valid. I concede all three.
[RESOLVED: All three — conceded by defender]

Verdict: critique_wins.
